abstract:
Recent progress in imitation learning has been enabled by policy
architectures that scale to complex visuomotor tasks, multimodal distributions,
and large datasets. However, these methods often rely on learning from large
amount of expert demonstrations. To address these shortcomings, we propose
Latent Diffusion Planning (LDP), a modular approach consisting of a planner
which can leverage action-free demonstrations, and an inverse dynamics model
which can leverage suboptimal data, that both operate over a learned latent
space. First, we learn a compact latent space through a variational
autoencoder, enabling effective forecasting of future states in image-based
domains. Then, we train a planner and an inverse dynamics model with diffusion
objectives. By separating planning from action prediction, LDP can benefit from
the denser supervision signals of suboptimal and action-free data. On simulated
visual robotic manipulation tasks, LDP outperforms state-of-the-art imitation
learning approaches, as they cannot leverage such additional data.

keyInsights: Dense forecasting in latent space outperforms hierarchical planning approaches
Combining suboptimal and action-free data leads to improved performance
Learned latent representations are more effective than fixed pretrained embeddings
originalProblem: Traditional imitation learning requires large amounts of expert demonstration data that is expensive and time-consuming to collect
Most existing methods cannot effectively utilize suboptimal trajectories or demonstrations without action labels
results: Successfully transfers learned policies to real-world robotic lifting tasks
Outperforms methods that cannot utilize action-free data
Achieves competitive or superior performance compared to existing imitation learning algorithms, especially with limited expert data
Demonstrates effective learning from both suboptimal trajectories and action-free demonstrations
solution: Learn a compact latent representation of visual observations using a VAE
Use diffusion models for both latent space planning and action prediction
Separate planning from action prediction through an inverse dynamics model
paper_summary.summary: Stanford and UC Berkeley researchers develop Latent Diffusion Planning (LDP), a framework that enables robots to learn control policies from both suboptimal trajectories and action-free demonstrations through latent space planning, achieving competitive performance on manipulation tasks while reducing dependence on expensive expert demonstrations.

title: Latent Diffusion Planning for Imitation Learning

abstract: World models paired with model predictive control (MPC) can be trained offline on large-scale datasets of expert trajectories and enable generalization to a wide range of planning tasks at inference time. Compared to traditional MPC procedures, which rely on slow search algorithms or on iteratively solving optimization problems exactly, gradient-based planning offers a computationally efficient alternative. However, the performance of gradient-based planning has thus far lagged behind that of other approaches. In this paper, we propose improved methods for training world models that enable efficient gradient-based planning. We begin with the observation that although a world model is trained on a next-state prediction objective, it is used at test-time to instead estimate a sequence of actions. The goal of our work is to close this train-test gap. To that end, we propose train-time data synthesis techniques that enable significantly improved gradient-based planning with existing world models. At test time, our approach outperforms or matches the classical gradient-free cross-entropy method (CEM) across a variety of object manipulation and navigation tasks in 10% of the time budget.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: Actively addressing the train-test gap through either online data expansion or adversarial robustness training is critical for unlocking the full potential of gradient-based planning.
Adversarial training can effectively smooth the action-level optimization landscape, making gradient-based methods more robust and efficient for continuous control tasks.

Combining powerful self-supervised visual encoders with specialized finetuning of latent dynamics models provides a robust framework for high-performance and computationally efficient robotic planning.
originalProblem: World models suffer from a "train-test gap" where they encounter out-of-distribution latent states during gradient-based planning, leading to compounding prediction errors.
The planning objective, derived from learned world models, often presents a complex, non-smooth loss landscape that hinders the effectiveness of gradient-based optimization.
Gradient-based planning has historically underperformed search-based methods empirically, despite its potential for computational efficiency in model-based control.
results: OWM and AWM substantially improved gradient-based planning success rates by up to +30% in open-loop settings, with AWM+Adam+MPC matching or exceeding the performance of search-based CEM.
The enhanced GBP performance was achieved with an order of magnitude (10x) reduction in computation time compared to the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM).

The finetuning methods effectively closed the train-test gap, reducing the world model error on planning trajectories and demonstrably smoothing the planning loss landscape for more stable optimization.
solution: **Online World Modeling (OWM)** iteratively expands the world model's training data by executing GBP-generated action sequences in the true environment simulator and adding the resulting "corrected" ground-truth trajectories.
**Adversarial World Modeling (AWM)** finetunes the world model by training it on adversarially perturbed latent states and actions, improving robustness and smoothing the planning loss landscape.
The approach leverages a fixed, pretrained DINOv2 self-supervised visual encoder to embed observations into a latent space, with a Vision Transformer serving as the learnable latent world model.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Columbia University and NYU introduced Online World Modeling (OWM) and Adversarial World Modeling (AWM) to mitigate the train-test gap in world models for gradient-based planning (GBP). These methods enabled GBP to achieve performance comparable to or better than search-based planning algorithms like CEM, while simultaneously reducing computation time by an order of magnitude across various robotic tasks.

title: Closing the Train-Test Gap in World Models for Gradient-Based Planning

abstract: Energy-based predictive world models provide a powerful approach for multi-step visual planning by reasoning over latent energy landscapes rather than generating pixels. However, existing approaches face two major challenges: (i) their latent representations are typically learned in Euclidean space, neglecting the underlying geometric and hierarchical structure among states, and (ii) they struggle with long-horizon prediction, which leads to rapid degradation across extended rollouts. To address these challenges, we introduce GeoWorld, a geometric world model that preserves geometric structure and hierarchical relations through a Hyperbolic JEPA, which maps latent representations from Euclidean space onto hyperbolic manifolds. We further introduce Geometric Reinforcement Learning for energy-based optimization, enabling stable multi-step planning in hyperbolic latent space. Extensive experiments on CrossTask and COIN demonstrate around 3% SR improvement in 3-step planning and 2% SR improvement in 4-step planning compared to the state-of-the-art V-JEPA 2. Project website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Incorporating hyperbolic geometry into latent spaces provides a powerful inductive bias that better captures hierarchical relationships and dynamics, enhancing the stability and accuracy of long-horizon visual planning.
Geometric Reinforcement Learning offers a principled method for optimizing predictors in non-Euclidean spaces by leveraging geometric properties and defining rewards based on hyperbolic distances.

A curvature-aware energy landscape generated by H-JEPA creates more structured and semantically meaningful representations of states, which is crucial for mitigating error accumulation in multi-step predictions.
originalProblem: Existing predictive world models typically learn latent representations in Euclidean space, which struggles to efficiently capture the intrinsic geometric and hierarchical structures of real-world states and their transitions.
Multi-step planning in current energy-based world models suffers from rapid performance degradation and error accumulation as the planning horizon increases, limiting applicability for complex tasks.
results: GeoWorld consistently outperforms V-JEPA 2 across benchmarks, showing approximately a 3% Success Rate improvement for 3-step planning and 2% for 4-step planning.

The model demonstrates superior long-horizon planning stability, maintaining significantly higher Success Rates across horizons up to T=8, effectively mitigating performance degradation due to accumulated geometric drift.

GeoWorld's advanced variant (ViT-g384) surpasses strong LLM-based planners, including InternVL3.5-241B and GPT-5, in visual planning with videos tasks, highlighting the benefits of explicit geometric reasoning.
solution: Introduces Hyperbolic JEPA (H-JEPA) to map visual observations onto a hyperbolic manifold, where geodesic distances inherently encode hierarchical relations for geometry-consistent latent dynamics.

Develops Geometric Reinforcement Learning (GRL), an energy-based optimization framework that refines the predictor by minimizing hyperbolic value functions and regularizing with the triangle inequality.
Utilizes the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) for energy-based planning, optimizing action sequences by minimizing the hyperbolic geodesic distance between predicted and target latent states.
paper_summary.summary: GeoWorld integrates hyperbolic geometry into energy-based predictive world models to improve long-horizon visual planning. The framework achieves up to a 3% increase in Success Rate for 3-step planning compared to V-JEPA 2, demonstrating superior stability over longer horizons and outperforming LLM-based planners in visual planning tasks.

title: GeoWorld: Geometric World Models

abstract:
Planning has been very successful for control tasks with known environment
dynamics. To leverage planning in unknown environments, the agent needs to
learn the dynamics from interactions with the world. However, learning dynamics
models that are accurate enough for planning has been a long-standing
challenge, especially in image-based domains. We propose the Deep Planning
Network (PlaNet), a purely model-based agent that learns the environment
dynamics from images and chooses actions through fast online planning in latent
space. To achieve high performance, the dynamics model must accurately predict
the rewards ahead for multiple time steps. We approach this using a latent
dynamics model with both deterministic and stochastic transition components.
Moreover, we propose a multi-step variational inference objective that we name
latent overshooting. Using only pixel observations, our agent solves continuous
control tasks with contact dynamics, partial observability, and sparse rewards,
which exceed the difficulty of tasks that were previously solved by planning
with learned models. PlaNet uses substantially fewer episodes and reaches final
performance close to and sometimes higher than strong model-free algorithms.

keyInsights: Combining deterministic (recurrent) and stochastic (latent state) components within the Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) is crucial for robustly learning dynamics from pixels, enabling both long-term memory and uncertainty modeling.
Online data collection guided by planning with the learned model is essential for data efficiency and performance on complex tasks, outperforming random exploration.

Effective planning in a compact latent space using Model Predictive Control (MPC) with the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) allows for efficient multi-step action optimization without requiring expensive pixel generation.
originalProblem: Model-based reinforcement learning (RL) struggled to learn accurate and robust dynamics models directly from high-dimensional pixel observations, limiting its application to complex continuous control tasks.

Existing model-based methods often faced challenges with accumulating prediction errors over long horizons, capturing environmental stochasticity and partial observability, and scaling to complex environments.

Model-free RL methods, while successful in vision-based tasks, were notoriously data-inefficient, requiring millions of environment interactions which is impractical for real-world applications like robotics.
results: PlaNet achieved performance on par with or superior to state-of-the-art model-free algorithms (e.g., D4PG, A3C) on challenging DeepMind Control Suite tasks, while being approximately 200x more data-efficient on average.

The Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) design was empirically validated, with the combination of deterministic and stochastic paths outperforming purely deterministic or purely stochastic model variants.

The learned latent dynamics model produced pixel-accurate predictions for up to 50 steps into the future and captured sufficient information to infer underlying simulator states, demonstrating high-fidelity world modeling from pixels.
solution: PlaNet, a purely model-based agent, iteratively learns environment dynamics from pixel observations and plans actions in a learned compact latent space.

It employs a novel Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) that combines a deterministic recurrent path for remembering long-term information with a stochastic latent state for capturing uncertainty and partial observability.

Actions are derived using Model Predictive Control (MPC) with the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) for planning within the learned latent space, optimizing future rewards over a planning horizon at each step.
paper_summary.summary: PlaNet, developed by researchers at Google Brain, DeepMind, and universities, introduces a purely model-based reinforcement learning agent capable of solving complex continuous control tasks directly from pixel observations. It leverages a novel Recurrent State-Space Model to learn latent dynamics and achieves data efficiency hundreds of times greater than leading model-free methods on tasks within the DeepMind Control Suite.

title: Learning Latent Dynamics for Planning from Pixels

abstract: World models simulate environment dynamics from raw sensory inputs like video. However, using them for planning can be challenging due to the vast and unstructured search space. We propose a robust and highly parallelizable planner that leverages the differentiability of the learned world model for efficient optimization, solving long-horizon control tasks from visual input. Our method treats states as optimization variables ("virtual states") with soft dynamics constraints, enabling parallel computation and easier optimization. To facilitate exploration and avoid local optima, we introduce stochasticity into the states. To mitigate sensitive gradients through high-dimensional vision-based world models, we modify the gradient structure to descend towards valid plans while only requiring action-input gradients. Our planner, which we call GRASP (Gradient RelAxed Stochastic Planner), can be viewed as a stochastic version of a non-condensed or collocation-based optimal controller. We provide theoretical justification and experiments on video-based world models, where our resulting planner outperforms existing planning algorithms like the cross-entropy method (CEM) and vanilla gradient-based optimization (GD) on long-horizon experiments, both in success rate and time to convergence.

keyInsights: Decoupling world model dynamics through "virtual states" allows for significant parallelization in planning, transforming the serial optimization challenge into a lifted problem.
Directly injecting Langevin-style noise into state iterates effectively facilitates exploration of the lifted state space, enabling escape from local minima and discovery of non-greedy solutions.

Gradients with respect to state inputs in vision-based world models are often brittle and can be exploited; detaching these gradients, combined with dense goal shaping, yields more robust and physically meaningful plans.
originalProblem: Existing planning methods struggle with long-horizon control tasks when leveraging learned visual world models, particularly in high-dimensional observation spaces.
Zero-order (sampling-based) algorithms face scalability issues due to serial rollouts and diminished performance with increasing planning horizons and action dimensions.
Traditional gradient-based methods are hindered by susceptibility to local minima and gradient instability arising from differentiating through long sequences of deep neural network dynamics.
results: GRASP achieves superior performance on long-horizon tasks, demonstrating a 43.4% success rate in the challenging Push-T environment at a 50-step horizon, outperforming CEM (30.2%), vanilla GD (37.6%), and Latent Collocation (4.2%).
The method consistently exhibits faster convergence and lower median planning times for successful trials across various environments, indicating enhanced computational efficiency.
Ablation studies confirm that the robust grad-cut dynamics and periodic full-rollout synchronization steps are critical components for GRASP's effectiveness in navigating complex loss landscapes.
solution: GRASP introduces parallelized planning using auxiliary "virtual states" alongside actions, reformulating the problem to allow parallel world model evaluations across the entire horizon.
It incorporates Langevin-style stochastic updates by injecting Gaussian noise directly into state iterates to encourage exploration and escape local minima in the non-convex optimization landscape.
A novel Grad-cut Dynamics Loss detaches gradients through world model state inputs to prevent brittle gradient exploitation, complemented by dense goal shaping for a global, task-aligned signal.
Periodic synchronization steps with full-gradient descent on the original serial rollout objective refine trajectories, balancing exploration with precise convergence.
paper_summary.summary: GRASP, a novel gradient-based planning algorithm, enables world models to effectively solve long-horizon control tasks from high-dimensional visual inputs by combining parallelized planning, Langevin-style exploration, and a robust gradient-cutting mechanism. It achieves a 43.4% success rate in the Push-T environment at a 50-step horizon, significantly outperforming prior methods.

title: Parallel Stochastic Gradient-Based Planning for World Models

abstract: The integration of pre-trained visual representations (PVRs) has significantly advanced visuomotor policy learning. However, effectively leveraging these models remains a challenge. We identify temporal entanglement as a critical, inherent issue when using these time-invariant models in sequential decision-making tasks. This entanglement arises because PVRs, optimised for static image understanding, struggle to represent the temporal dependencies crucial for visuomotor control. In this work, we quantify the impact of temporal entanglement, demonstrating a strong correlation between a policy's success rate and the ability of its latent space to capture task-progression cues. Based on these insights, we propose a simple, yet effective disentanglement baseline designed to mitigate temporal entanglement. Our empirical results show that traditional methods aimed at enriching features with temporal components are insufficient on their own, highlighting the necessity of explicitly addressing temporal disentanglement for robust visuomotor policy learning.

keyInsights: Temporal entanglement, where PVRs fail to differentiate visually similar yet temporally distinct states, is a fundamental limitation directly impacting visuomotor policy success.
The ability of a PVR's latent space to perceive task progression (measured by task-progression loss) strongly correlates with both temporal entanglement and downstream policy performance.

Explicit task-progression signals, like the proposed Temporal Encoding, are more effective in mitigating temporal entanglement than implicit temporal modeling or simple feature augmentation, even benefiting PVRs with existing temporal training.
originalProblem: Pre-trained visual representations (PVRs), despite excelling in static image tasks, often underperform in visuomotor control, where temporal dependencies are crucial for sequential decision-making.

PVRs, optimized for static images, struggle to capture the temporal progression necessary for robotic tasks, leading to 'temporal entanglement' where features from distinct time steps become indistinguishable.
The robustness of PVRs to minor visual changes, beneficial for static tasks, paradoxically hinders performance in robot learning where subtle temporal changes convey significant state information.
results: A strong negative correlation was found between short/long-range temporal entanglement metrics and policy success rate, establishing entanglement as a key performance bottleneck.

The Temporal Encoding (TE) baseline significantly boosted average policy success rate by over 7% (from 56.11% to 63.35%) across 14 diverse PVRs, and by +20% when combined with a Causal Transformer policy.

TE consistently outperformed feature augmentation methods (FLARE) and improved performance even for PVRs explicitly trained with temporal objectives (e.g., R3M, VIP), while dedicated Video-PVRs showed limited efficacy for visuomotor tasks in this context.
solution: Quantified temporal entanglement using novel metrics: average sequential cosine similarity for short-range entanglement, average global cosine similarity for long-range entanglement, and a task-progression probing method.

Proposed a Temporal Encoding (TE) baseline that injects an explicit task-progression signal (timestep index encoded as a high-dimensional vector) into the PVR latent space, augmenting the policy input.

Evaluated the TE baseline against conventional temporal integration strategies, including feature augmentation (e.g., FLARE), policy-based methods (Causal Transformers), and PVRs explicitly pre-trained with temporal objectives or video data.
paper_summary.summary: This research identifies and quantifies "temporal entanglement" as a critical challenge in utilizing pre-trained visual representations (PVRs) for visuomotor policy learning, where PVRs struggle to distinguish visually similar but temporally distinct states. The work proposes a simple Temporal Encoding (TE) baseline that explicitly augments PVR features with a task-progression signal, achieving over a 7% increase in average policy success rate across various PVRs and tasks.

title: The Temporal Trap: Entanglement in Pre-Trained Visual Representations for Visuomotor Policy Learning

abstract: Visual navigation requires agents to reach goals in complex environments through perception and planning. World models address this task by simulating action-conditioned state transitions to predict future observations. Current navigation world models typically learn state evolution under actions within the compressed latent space of a Variational Autoencoder, where spatial compression often discards fine-grained structural information and hinders precise control. To better understand the propagation characteristics of different representations, we conduct a linear dynamics probe and observe that dense DINOv2 features exhibit stronger linear predictability for action-conditioned transitions. Motivated by this observation, we propose the Representation Autoencoder-based Navigation World Model (RAE-NWM), which models navigation dynamics in a dense visual representation space. We employ a Conditional Diffusion Transformer with Decoupled Diffusion Transformer head (CDiT-DH) to model continuous transitions, and introduce a separate time-driven gating module for dynamics conditioning to regulate action injection strength during generation. Extensive evaluations show that modeling sequential rollouts in this space improves structural stability and action accuracy, benefiting downstream planning and navigation.

keyInsights: Dense visual representations from DINOv2 exhibit substantially stronger linear predictability for action-conditioned state transitions compared to compressed VAE-based latents.
The preservation of spatial structure within DINOv2 features, not just global semantics, is crucial for accurate action-conditioned dynamics modeling.

Adaptive modulation of dynamics conditioning, stronger in early high-noise stages and relaxed in late low-noise stages, is critical for maintaining long-horizon geometric consistency and action accuracy.
originalProblem: Existing Navigation World Models (NWMs) often rely on VAEs, which discard fine-grained structural and geometric information during spatial compression.
This information loss leads to significant structural degradation and kinematic deviations during long-horizon future predictions.
The resulting unreliable predictions compromise the effectiveness and safety of downstream path planning for visual navigation systems.
results: RAE-NWM achieved lower LPIPS (0.349 vs 0.470) and FID (15.90 vs 33.06) scores at a 16-second direct prediction horizon compared to the NWM baseline.
Improved planning accuracy on the SACSoN dataset, achieving a lower Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE 2.91 vs 4.12) and Relative Pose Error (RPE 0.70 vs 0.96).
Demonstrated a Success Rate of 78.95% in closed-loop image-goal navigation within the Habitat simulator, outperforming several other visual navigation methods.
solution: The model extracts state representations directly from dense, uncompressed DINOv2 patch tokens, avoiding the information loss associated with VAEs.
A Conditional Diffusion Transformer with a Decoupled Diffusion Transformer head (CDiT-DH) serves as the generative backbone, modeling environment dynamics within this high-dimensional space.

An adaptive, time-driven gating function dynamically modulates the strength of action signal injection throughout the continuous probability flow, balancing kinematic guidance and visual detail refinement.
paper_summary.summary: RAE-NWM introduces a Navigation World Model that operates within dense DINOv2 visual representation spaces, mitigating structural degradation and kinematic deviations common in VAE-based models. This approach achieves improved long-horizon prediction fidelity, outperforming baselines with lower LPIPS and FID scores, and enhances planning accuracy in navigation tasks with reduced Absolute Trajectory Error.

title: RAE-NWM: Navigation World Model in Dense Visual Representation Space

abstract:
Visual Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) promises to encapsulate
agent's knowledge about the underlying dynamics of the environment, enabling
learning a world model as a useful planner. However, top MBRL agents such as
Dreamer often struggle with visual pixel-based inputs in the presence of
exogenous or irrelevant noise in the observation space, due to failure to
capture task-specific features while filtering out irrelevant spatio-temporal
details. To tackle this problem, we apply a spatio-temporal masking strategy, a
bisimulation principle, combined with latent reconstruction, to capture
endogenous task-specific aspects of the environment for world models,
effectively eliminating non-essential information. Joint training of
representations, dynamics, and policy often leads to instabilities. To further
address this issue, we develop a Hybrid Recurrent State-Space Model (HRSSM)
structure, enhancing state representation robustness for effective policy
learning. Our empirical evaluation demonstrates significant performance
improvements over existing methods in a range of visually complex control tasks
such as Maniskill \cite{gu2023maniskill2} with exogenous distractors from the
Matterport environment. Our code is avaliable at
https://github.com/bit1029public/HRSSM.

keyInsights: Effective world models can learn expressive latent states for planning without requiring pixel-level reconstruction, enhancing robustness to visual noise.
The combination of spatio-temporal masking for redundancy reduction and a bisimulation principle for task-relevant information capture is synergistic and critical for robust representation learning.
The Hybrid Recurrent State-Space Model (HRSSM) architecture, using dual branches with EMA updates, provides a stable and efficient framework for co-training these objectives.
originalProblem: Leading visual Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) agents struggle to learn effective world models from visually complex and noisy observations.
Reliance on pixel-level reconstruction forces models to focus on irrelevant details, hindering the capture of task-specific features and leading to compounded errors.
Prior attempts to handle exogenous noise, such as decoder-free approaches, often fail to capture enough task-specific information or exhibit insufficient robustness.
results: The HRSSM consistently outperformed DreamerV3 and other baselines on standard and distracted DeepMind Control tasks, showing superior sample efficiency and final performance.
It achieved highly competitive results on realistic ManiSkill environments (e.g., ManiSkill's `Turn Faucet` where it substantially outperformed DreamerV3 with 278±97 vs. 138±83 score).

The model demonstrated robust generalization, maintaining high performance across nine different types of visual distractions, and Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed its ability to focus on task-relevant objects while filtering out background noise.
Despite its complexity, HRSSM's wall-clock training time was comparable to DreamerV3 due to the decoder-free approach and efficient HRSSM design.
solution: A Hybrid Recurrent State-Space Model (HRSSM) is introduced, which learns robust latent representations for model-based agents without pixel-level reconstruction.
The HRSSM employs a spatio-temporal masking strategy combined with latent reconstruction to reduce visual redundancy and enforce semantic alignment between masked and raw observations.
A bisimulation-based similarity loss is integrated to ensure the learned representations are sensitive to task-relevant rewards and dynamics.
A dual-branch architecture with Exponential Moving Average (EMA) updates for one branch maintains training stability and computational efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Beijing Institute of Technology and DreamFold AI developed a Hybrid Recurrent State-Space Model (HRSSM) that learns robust, task-specific latent representations for model-based reinforcement learning agents without relying on pixel-level reconstruction. This approach significantly outperforms prior methods like DreamerV3 on visually distracted DeepMind Control tasks and real-world ManiSkill environments while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Learning Latent Dynamic Robust Representations for World Models

abstract: Learning predictive world models from raw visual observations is a central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), especially for robotics and continuous control. Conventional model-based RL frameworks directly condition future predictions on absolute actions, which makes optimization unstable: the optimal action distributions are task-dependent, unknown a priori, and often lead to oscillatory or inefficient control. To address this, we introduce the Residual-Action World Model (ResWM), a new framework that reformulates the control variable from absolute actions to residual actions -- incremental adjustments relative to the previous step. This design aligns with the inherent smoothness of real-world control, reduces the effective search space, and stabilizes long-horizon planning. To further strengthen the representation, we propose an Observation Difference Encoder that explicitly models the changes between adjacent frames, yielding compact latent dynamics that are naturally coupled with residual actions. ResWM is integrated into a Dreamer-style latent dynamics model with minimal modifications and no extra hyperparameters. Both imagination rollouts and policy optimization are conducted in the residual-action space, enabling smoother exploration, lower control variance, and more reliable planning. Empirical results on the DeepMind Control Suite demonstrate that ResWM achieves consistent improvements in sample efficiency, asymptotic returns, and control smoothness, significantly surpassing strong baselines such as Dreamer and TD-MPC. Beyond performance, ResWM produces more stable and energy-efficient action trajectories, a property critical for robotic systems deployed in real-world environments. These findings suggest that residual action modeling provides a simple yet powerful principle for bridging algorithmic advances in RL with the practical requirements of robotics.

keyInsights: Modeling actions as incremental residuals simplifies the optimization landscape from a global search to localized adjustments, promoting stable, energy-efficient control trajectories.

Explicitly encoding the difference between consecutive visual observations helps distill dynamic information, filtering out static background noise and fostering a more compact, dynamics-aware latent state.

A unified framework that combines residual action reparameterization with dynamics-aware observation encoding within a world model can mitigate compounding errors and enhance the fidelity of imagined trajectories for policy learning.
originalProblem: Traditional model-based RL often conditions latent dynamics on absolute actions, leading to optimization instability, erratic control, and a lack of inherent temporal smoothness in continuous control tasks.

Current visual encoders frequently process static frames independently, which can introduce redundancy and obscure the explicit temporal dynamics crucial for understanding action-induced state changes.
Model-based reinforcement learning agents typically struggle with sample inefficiency, limiting their practical application in real-world scenarios where data acquisition is costly or risky.
results: ResWM consistently improved performance across various baselines, boosting the average score of DeepRAD by approximately 21% (from 695.1 to 828.7) at 100K steps on DMControl tasks.

Achieved the highest average scores on DMControl standard (828.7 at 100K steps, 925.0 at 500K steps) and hard tasks (435.6 at 500K steps, 644.8 at 1M steps), outperforming methods like ResAct and TACO.
Demonstrated strong generalization on the Atari benchmark, achieving the highest normalized mean score of 0.96 and leading in raw scores across all ten tested games.
solution: The policy is reparameterized to predict residual actions, incremental adjustments ($\delta a_t$) relative to the previous action ($a_{t-1}$), to enforce temporal smoothness and stabilize optimization.
An Observation Difference Encoder (ODL) is introduced to map consecutive visual frames ($o_t, o_{t-1}$) to a dynamics-aware latent representation, explicitly modeling temporal deltas.

These components are integrated into a Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM), where the transition function is conditioned directly on the residual action ($\delta a_t$), and policy learning utilizes imagination-based rollouts with regularization.
paper_summary.summary: The Residual-Action World Model (ResWM) redefines action and observation representations in latent variable world models by using residual actions for smoother control and an Observation Difference Encoder for dynamics-aware latent states. This approach consistently achieved higher average scores on DMControl benchmarks (e.g., 925.0 on standard tasks at 500K steps) and led on the Atari benchmark with a normalized mean score of 0.96, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and stability.

title: ResWM: Residual-Action World Model for Visual RL

abstract: World model based planning has significantly improved decision-making in complex environments by enabling agents to simulate future states and make informed choices. This computational burden is particularly restrictive in robotics, where resources are severely constrained. To address this limitation, we propose a Sparse Imagination for Efficient Visual World Model Planning, which enhances computational efficiency by reducing the number of tokens processed during forward prediction. Our method leverages a sparsely trained vision-based world model based on transformers with randomized grouped attention strategy, allowing the model to flexibly adjust the number of tokens processed based on the computational resource. By enabling sparse imagination during latent rollout, our approach significantly accelerates planning while maintaining high control fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that sparse imagination preserves task performance while dramatically improving inference efficiency. This general technique for visual planning is applicable from simple test-time trajectory optimization to complex real-world tasks with the latest VLAs, enabling the deployment of world models in real-time scenarios.
title: Sparse Imagination for Efficient Visual World Model Planning

abstract: Planning-based reinforcement learning has shown strong performance in tasks in discrete and low-dimensional continuous action spaces. However, planning usually brings significant computational overhead for decision-making, and scaling such methods to high-dimensional action spaces remains challenging. To advance efficient planning for high-dimensional continuous control, we propose Trajectory Autoencoding Planner (TAP), which learns low-dimensional latent action codes with a state-conditional VQ-VAE. The decoder of the VQ-VAE thus serves as a novel dynamics model that takes latent actions and current state as input and reconstructs long-horizon trajectories. During inference time, given a starting state, TAP searches over discrete latent actions to find trajectories that have both high probability under the training distribution and high predicted cumulative reward. Empirical evaluation in the offline RL setting demonstrates low decision latency which is indifferent to the growing raw action dimensionality. For Adroit robotic hand manipulation tasks with high-dimensional continuous action space, TAP surpasses existing model-based methods by a large margin and also beats strong model-free actor-critic baselines.
title: Efficient Planning in a Compact Latent Action Space

abstract: Building deep learning models that can reason about their environment requires capturing its underlying dynamics. Joint-Embedded Predictive Architectures (JEPA) provide a promising framework to model such dynamics by learning representations and predictors through a self-supervised prediction objective. However, their ability to support effective action planning remains limited. We propose an approach to enhance planning with JEPA world models by shaping their representation space so that the negative goal-conditioned value function for a reaching cost in a given environment is approximated by a distance (or quasi-distance) between state embeddings. We introduce a practical method to enforce this constraint during training and show that it leads to significantly improved planning performance compared to standard JEPA models on simple control tasks.

keyInsights: Explicitly shaping the JEPA's latent space with a value function objective, where distances approximate negative values, significantly enhances planning performance by providing a smoother, more guided cost landscape.

Using a quasi-distance for value function approximation consistently leads to superior planning accuracy across environments, suggesting its effectiveness in capturing the potentially asymmetric nature of optimal value functions.

Training the value-aligned state encoder separately from the action encoder and predictor is generally more effective than joint training, indicating potential conflicts between the self-supervised prediction objective and the value function objective.
originalProblem: Joint-Embedded Predictive Architectures (JEPA) show promise for learning predictive representations but have limitations in supporting effective action planning.
Planning with world models often faces challenges due to complex and misleading cost landscapes in the learned representation space, leading to suboptimal plans caused by numerous local minima.
Arbitrarily learned latent spaces do not inherently encode a clear notion of "cost-to-go" or "value-to-goal," making optimization for goal-reaching difficult.
results: Value-guided JEPA methods consistently achieved higher planning accuracy compared to standard prediction-based JEPA baselines and other representation learning approaches.

The `VF quasi` approach, which integrates a quasi-distance into the value function, demonstrated the best performance, reaching 0.96 planning accuracy in the WB environment and 0.63 in the Maze environment.

Combining the value function objective with the prediction loss (`VF quasi pred`) generally resulted in decreased performance (e.g., 0.85 in WB vs. 0.96 for `VF quasi`), highlighting the benefits of a dedicated, separately trained value-aligned state encoder.
solution: The proposed method shapes the JEPA's state encoder representation space by defining a value function as the negative squared distance between embedded states and goals.
Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) is utilized to train this value-aligned representation from offline, reward-free trajectories, making the latent space directly amenable to planning optimization.
A quasi-distance is explored instead of a standard Euclidean distance for the value function approximation, allowing for asymmetry to better capture the true nature of goal-reaching costs.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces an approach to enhance action planning in Joint-Embedded Predictive Architectures (JEPA) by integrating goal-conditioned value function learning directly into their representation space. The method structures the JEPA's latent space such that distances approximate negative value functions, leading to improved planning accuracy, particularly achieving up to 0.96 accuracy in specific navigation tasks when employing a quasi-distance metric.

title: Value-guided action planning with JEPA world models

abstract:
To achieve autonomy in a priori unknown real-world scenarios, agents should
be able to: i) act from high-dimensional sensory observations (e.g., images),
ii) learn from past experience to adapt and improve, and iii) be capable of
long horizon planning. Classical planning algorithms (e.g. PRM, RRT) are
proficient at handling long-horizon planning. Deep learning based methods in
turn can provide the necessary representations to address the others, by
modeling statistical contingencies between observations. In this direction, we
introduce a general-purpose planning algorithm called PALMER that combines
classical sampling-based planning algorithms with learning-based perceptual
representations. For training these perceptual representations, we combine
Q-learning with contrastive representation learning to create a latent space
where the distance between the embeddings of two states captures how easily an
optimal policy can traverse between them. For planning with these perceptual
representations, we re-purpose classical sampling-based planning algorithms to
retrieve previously observed trajectory segments from a replay buffer and
restitch them into approximately optimal paths that connect any given pair of
start and goal states. This creates a tight feedback loop between
representation learning, memory, reinforcement learning, and sampling-based
planning. The end result is an experiential framework for long-horizon planning
that is significantly more robust and sample efficient compared to existing
methods.

keyInsights: Effective long-horizon planning requires both perceptual understanding and the ability to leverage past experiences, which PALMER achieves by combining learned representations with memory-based retrieval.

Learning perceptual representations that explicitly encode the notion of reachability between states (guided by Q-learning and contrastive learning) enables efficient retrieval of relevant experiences.
Classical sampling-based planning algorithms can be adapted to work with retrieved trajectory segments from memory, allowing for robust trajectory stitching and long-horizon path generation.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents struggle with long-horizon planning in complex, unknown environments when relying solely on high-dimensional sensory observations.
Traditional planning algorithms require explicit environment models, while deep learning methods often lack robust long-term reasoning capabilities.
Existing methods for learning-based planning face challenges with sample efficiency and generalization to unseen environments over extended time horizons.
results: PALMER consistently outperformed baseline methods (SPTM, SoRB) in terms of success rate across various goal distances in environments like ViZDoom, Habitat, and Maze2D.
A model trained on data from a single apartment in Habitat demonstrated generalization capabilities to unseen apartments, indicating robust perceptual representations.
The framework showed scalability with increasing goal distances and improved sample efficiency, effectively planning and navigating with limited exploration data.
solution: PALMER integrates a perceptual encoder to map observations into a latent space capturing reachability, an experience memory, and a perceptual experience retrieval mechanism.
It learns perceptual representations by combining goal-conditioned Q-learning with contrastive representation learning, ensuring states with high reachability are close in the latent space.
A trajectory stitching algorithm, adapting classical planning methods like PRM/RRT, constructs paths by combining retrieved segments from memory, which is continually refined with new experiences.
paper_summary.summary: PALMER, developed by researchers at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (EPFL), is a framework enabling autonomous agents to perform long-horizon planning using only high-dimensional sensory observations. It combines perceptual representation learning with memory-based experience retrieval and trajectory stitching, demonstrating superior success rates over baselines and generalizing to unseen environments.

title: PALMER: Perception-Action Loop with Memory for Long-Horizon Planning

abstract: World models enable planning in imagined future predicted space, offering a promising framework for embodied navigation. However, existing navigation world models often lack action-conditioned consistency, so visually plausible predictions can still drift under multi-step rollout and degrade planning. Moreover, efficient deployment requires few-step diffusion inference, but existing distillation methods do not explicitly preserve rollout consistency, creating a training-inference mismatch. To address these challenges, we propose MWM, a mobile world model for planning-based image-goal navigation. Specifically, we introduce a two-stage training framework that combines structure pretraining with Action-Conditioned Consistency (ACC) post-training to improve action-conditioned rollout consistency. We further introduce Inference-Consistent State Distillation (ICSD) for few-step diffusion distillation with improved rollout consistency. Our experiments on benchmark and real-world tasks demonstrate consistent gains in visual fidelity, trajectory accuracy, planning success, and inference efficiency. Code: this https URL. Website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Decomposing world model training into distinct structural learning and consistency refinement stages effectively balances visual fidelity with multi-step autoregressive accuracy.
Explicitly training for action-conditioned consistency using self-forcing and perceptual losses significantly reduces error accumulation in multi-step rollouts.

Introducing an 'inference-consistent state' during distillation helps preserve sequence consistency when accelerating diffusion models, addressing the mismatch between training and efficient inference.
originalProblem: Existing world models for embodied navigation often produce visually plausible but 'action-conditioned inconsistent' rollouts, where imagined trajectories deviate from true physical paths, undermining Model Predictive Control (MPC).

Diffusion models, while good for visual generation, typically have high inference latency (many denoising steps) which is impractical for real-time MPC on robots, and current acceleration methods do not explicitly preserve action-conditioned consistency.
results: MWM (with 5 DDIM steps) demonstrates superior action-conditioned consistency on the SCAND dataset, achieving lower LPIPS (0.495) and DreamSim (0.337) at 16s horizon compared to NWM (25 DDIM steps, LPIPS 0.569, DreamSim 0.373).
It achieves a 4x speedup in average rollout time (2.3s vs. 9.6s for NWM) on an NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 GPU, while maintaining or improving visual fidelity (lower FID scores).

On real-world goal-image navigation tasks with the AIRBOT MMK2 platform, MWM achieves a higher Success Rate of 0.30 and lower Navigation Error of 2.16, outperforming baselines like NoMaD (SR 0.08, NE 2.88) and NWM (SR 0.20, NE 3.18).
solution: A two-stage 'Structure-First, Consistency-Refine' training pipeline is introduced, pretraining a diffusion model for scene structure and then post-training to mitigate error accumulation and improve action-conditioned consistency.

Inference-Consistent State Distillation (ICSD) is designed to enable efficient, few-step diffusion inference while explicitly preserving consistency, by bridging the gap between truncated training states and inference-time denoising endpoints.

The model integrates with Model Predictive Control (MPC) using the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) to search for optimal action sequences, evaluated by a terminal-frame perceptual objective against a goal image.
paper_summary.summary: MWM (Mobile World Models) improves visual planning for robot navigation by training a world model to generate action-conditioned future observations with enhanced consistency and faster inference. The system achieves a 4x speedup in rollout time while improving multi-step rollout quality and leads to better goal-image navigation performance in real-world scenarios.

title: MWM: Mobile World Models for Action-Conditioned Consistent Prediction

abstract: World models learned from high-dimensional visual observations allow agents to make decisions and plan directly in latent space, avoiding pixel-level reconstruction. However, recent latent predictive architectures (JEPAs), including the DINO world model (DINO-WM), display a degradation in test time robustness due to their sensitivity to "slow features". These include visual variations such as background changes and distractors that are irrelevant to the task being solved. We address this limitation by augmenting the predictive objective with a bisimulation encoder that enforces control-relevant state equivalence, mapping states with similar transition dynamics to nearby latent states while limiting contributions from slow features. We evaluate our model on a simple navigation task under different test-time background changes and visual distractors. Across all benchmarks, our model consistently improves robustness to slow features while operating in a reduced latent space, up to 10x smaller than that of DINO-WM. Moreover, our model is agnostic to the choice of pretrained visual encoder and maintains robustness when paired with DINOv2, SimDINOv2, and iBOT features.

keyInsights: Reward-free bisimulation, relying solely on transition dynamics, can effectively induce control-relevant invariant representations without requiring explicit reward signals.
The learned invariant latent space can be significantly more compact, demonstrating that increased robustness does not necessitate higher dimensional representations.
The approach is agnostic to the choice of pretrained visual encoder, effectively improving robustness across various state-of-the-art self-supervised visual backbones.
originalProblem: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) for world models are sensitive to "slow features" (e.g., background textures, lighting, camera viewpoints) that are visually consistent but irrelevant for task planning.
This sensitivity leads to a degradation of test-time robustness when visual conditions shift, causing planning failures even for identical core dynamics.

Existing large visual foundation models, while semantically rich, still extract control-irrelevant information, which can entangle task-relevant states with nuisance features in learned latent dynamics.
results: DINO-Bisim maintained high success rates (0.76-0.86) across various background changes and moving distractors in the PointMaze task, significantly outperforming DINO-WM (0.48-0.72) under similar shifts.
The method achieved enhanced robustness while operating in a latent space approximately 10 times smaller than that of the original DINO-WM.
The bisimulation-based invariance mechanism generalized across different pretrained visual encoders (DINOv2, SimDINOv2, iBOT), consistently improving their robustness to visual distribution shifts.
solution: A bisimulation encoder is trained on top of frozen, pretrained visual feature extractors (e.g., DINOv2) to map high-dimensional visual embeddings into a lower-dimensional, control-relevant latent space.
A reward-free bisimulation loss enforces that states with similar future transition dynamics are mapped to nearby latent states, actively limiting the contribution of slow features.

PCA-based Variance Covariance Regularization (PCA-VICReg) is employed to selectively allow collapse along principal component directions correlated with dominant nuisance features, while maintaining variance in other directions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Columbia University introduce DINO-Bisim, a method that learns invariant visual representations for planning by integrating a bisimulation encoder into Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs). This approach enhances world model robustness against task-irrelevant visual changes like background alterations and distractors, maintaining high planning success rates in challenging environments while reducing latent space dimensionality by a factor of 10.

title: Learning Invariant Visual Representations for Planning with Joint-Embedding Predictive World Models

abstract: In tasks aiming for long-term returns, planning becomes essential. We study generative modeling for planning with datasets repurposed from offline reinforcement learning. Specifically, we identify temporal consistency in the absence of step-wise rewards as one key technical challenge. We introduce the Latent Plan Transformer (LPT), a novel model that leverages a latent variable to connect a Transformer-based trajectory generator and the final return. LPT can be learned with maximum likelihood estimation on trajectory-return pairs. In learning, posterior sampling of the latent variable naturally integrates sub-trajectories to form a consistent abstraction despite the finite context. At test time, the latent variable is inferred from an expected return before policy execution, realizing the idea of planning as inference. Our experiments demonstrate that LPT can discover improved decisions from sub-optimal trajectories, achieving competitive performance across several benchmarks, including Gym-Mujoco, Franka Kitchen, Maze2D, and Connect Four. It exhibits capabilities in nuanced credit assignments, trajectory stitching, and adaptation to environmental contingencies. These results validate that latent variable inference can be a strong alternative to step-wise reward prompting.
title: Latent Plan Transformer for Trajectory Abstraction: Planning as Latent Space Inference

abstract: Developing agents that can perform complex control tasks from high-dimensional observations is a core ability of autonomous agents that requires underlying robust task control policies and adapting the underlying visual representations to the task. Most existing policies need a lot of training samples and treat this problem from the lens of two-stage learning with a controller learned on top of pre-trained vision models. We approach this problem from the lens of Koopman theory and learn visual representations from robotic agents conditioned on specific downstream tasks in the context of learning stabilizing control for the agent. We introduce a Contrastive Spectral Koopman Embedding network that allows us to learn efficient linearized visual representations from the agent's visual data in a high dimensional latent space and utilizes reinforcement learning to perform off-policy control on top of the extracted representations with a linear controller. Our method enhances stability and control in gradient dynamics over time, significantly outperforming existing approaches by improving efficiency and accuracy in learning task policies over extended horizons.
title: RoboKoop: Efficient Control Conditioned Representations from Visual Input in Robotics using Koopman Operator

abstract: Agents navigating in 3D environments require some form of memory, which should hold a compact and actionable representation of the history of observations useful for decision taking and planning. In most end-to-end learning approaches the representation is latent and usually does not have a clearly defined interpretation, whereas classical robotics addresses this with scene reconstruction resulting in some form of map, usually estimated with geometry and sensor models and/or learning. In this work we propose to learn an actionable representation of the scene independently of the targeted downstream task and without explicitly optimizing reconstruction. The learned representation is optimized by a blind auxiliary agent trained to navigate with it on multiple short sub episodes branching out from a waypoint and, most importantly, without any direct visual observation. We argue and show that the blindness property is important and forces the (trained) latent representation to be the only means for planning. With probing experiments we show that the learned representation optimizes navigability and not reconstruction. On downstream tasks we show that it is robust to changes in distribution, in particular the sim2real gap, which we evaluate with a real physical robot in a real office building, significantly improving performance.
title: Learning with a Mole: Transferable latent spatial representations for navigation without reconstruction

abstract: World models enable robots to conduct counterfactual reasoning in physical environments by predicting future world states. While conventional approaches often prioritize pixel-level reconstruction of future scenes, such detailed rendering is computationally intensive and unnecessary for planning tasks like navigation. We therefore propose that prediction and planning can be efficiently performed directly within a latent space of high-level semantic representations. To realize this, we introduce the Representative Latent space Navigation World Model (ReL-NWM). Rather than relying on reconstructionoriented latent embeddings, our method leverages a pre-trained representation encoder, DINOv3, and incorporates specialized mechanisms to effectively integrate action signals and historical context within this representation space. By operating entirely in the latent domain, our model bypasses expensive explicit reconstruction and achieves highly efficient navigation planning. Experiments show state-of-the-art trajectory prediction and image-goal navigation performance on multiple benchmarks. Additionally, we demonstrate real-world applicability by deploying the system on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot, confirming its efficiency and robustness in practical navigation scenarios.

keyInsights: Pixel-level reconstruction is unnecessary for effective image-goal navigation; instead, leveraging high-level semantic features from foundation models like DINOv3 provides superior and more efficient representations for planning.

Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) offers a more effective approach for action conditioning in world models than simple concatenation, resulting in stronger action-state coupling and improved predictive dynamics.

Efficient long-horizon planning is achievable by compressing spatiotemporal sequences with modules like TokenLearner and refining predictions using SpatioTemporal Cross-Attention to retain crucial historical context.
originalProblem: Existing world models for navigation are computationally intensive due to pixel-level reconstruction, leading to high inference latency unsuitable for real-time onboard robot deployment.
Traditional reconstruction-oriented latent spaces, such as those from VAEs, often fail to adequately capture high-level semantic cues critical for robust navigation tasks.
End-to-end learning frameworks struggle with adapting to complex, dynamic, or unstructured real-world scenarios due to limitations in current world model architectures.
results: ReL-NWM achieved state-of-the-art trajectory prediction accuracy on the RECON dataset (ATE 1.09, RPE 0.30) and the HuRoN dataset (ATE 3.59, RPE 0.93), outperforming existing baselines.

In Habitat-Sim, the system demonstrated superior image-goal navigation with a 60% Success Rate and 48.5% Success weighted by Path Length, achieving a 36x speedup in inference time over NWM (1.49s vs 55.31s per planning step).

Real-world deployment on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot with onboard NVIDIA Jetson Orin hardware showed online planning and navigation capabilities, with a 66.7% success rate for a challenging long-corridor task.
solution: ReL-NWM develops a lightweight, reconstruction-free latent world model that operates entirely within a representative latent space, utilizing a pre-trained DINOv3 vision encoder for semantically rich visual features.

The model integrates a novel Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) mechanism for strong action conditioning and SpatioTemporal Cross-Attention to effectively incorporate historical context into future state predictions.

The world model is trained with multi-step autoregressive supervision and a composite loss function, and an Image-goal navigation strategy based on Model Predictive Control (MPC) directly compares goal and predicted states in the latent space.
paper_summary.summary: ReL-NWM, developed by researchers at the National University of Defense Technology, introduces an efficient image-goal navigation system that leverages a reconstruction-free latent world model using DINOv3 features for real-time planning. The approach achieves a 36x speedup over previous methods in simulation with an average inference time of 1.49 seconds per planning step and demonstrates successful real-world navigation on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot with a 66.7% success rate.

title: Efficient Image-Goal Navigation with Representative Latent World Model

abstract:
A key challenge in complex visuomotor control is learning abstract
representations that are effective for specifying goals, planning, and
generalization. To this end, we introduce universal planning networks (UPN).
UPNs embed differentiable planning within a goal-directed policy. This planning
computation unrolls a forward model in a latent space and infers an optimal
action plan through gradient descent trajectory optimization. The
plan-by-gradient-descent process and its underlying representations are learned
end-to-end to directly optimize a supervised imitation learning objective. We
find that the representations learned are not only effective for goal-directed
visual imitation via gradient-based trajectory optimization, but can also
provide a metric for specifying goals using images. The learned representations
can be leveraged to specify distance-based rewards to reach new target states
for model-free reinforcement learning, resulting in substantially more
effective learning when solving new tasks described via image-based goals. We
were able to achieve successful transfer of visuomotor planning strategies
across robots with significantly different morphologies and actuation
capabilities.
title: Universal Planning Networks

abstract:
We present Planning as Descent (PaD), a framework for offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning that grounds trajectory synthesis in verification. Instead of learning a policy or explicit planner, PaD learns a goal-conditioned energy function over entire latent trajectories, assigning low energy to feasible, goal-consistent futures. Planning is realized as gradient-based refinement in this energy landscape, using identical computation during training and inference to reduce train-test mismatch common in decoupled modeling pipelines.
PaD is trained via self-supervised hindsight goal relabeling, shaping the energy landscape around the planning dynamics. At inference, multiple trajectory candidates are refined under different temporal hypotheses, and low-energy plans balancing feasibility and efficiency are selected.
We evaluate PaD on OGBench cube manipulation tasks. When trained on narrow expert demonstrations, PaD achieves state-of-the-art 95\% success, strongly outperforming prior methods that peak at 68\%. Remarkably, training on noisy, suboptimal data further improves success and plan efficiency, highlighting the benefits of verification-driven planning. Our results suggest learning to evaluate and refine trajectories provides a robust alternative to direct policy learning for offline, reward-free planning.
title: Planning as Descent: Goal-Conditioned Latent Trajectory Synthesis in Learned Energy Landscapes

abstract: Effective exploration in reinforcement learning requires not only tracking where an agent has been, but also understanding how the agent perceives and represents the world. To learn powerful representations, an agent should actively explore states that contribute to its knowledge of the environment. Temporal representations can capture the information necessary to solve a wide range of potential tasks while avoiding the computational cost associated with full state reconstruction. In this paper, we propose an exploration method that leverages temporal contrastive representations to guide exploration, prioritizing states with unpredictable future outcomes. We demonstrate that such representations can enable the learning of complex exploratory x in locomotion, manipulation, and embodied-AI tasks, revealing capabilities and behaviors that traditionally require extrinsic rewards. Unlike approaches that rely on explicit distance learning or episodic memory mechanisms (e.g., quasimetric-based methods), our method builds directly on temporal similarities, yielding a simpler yet effective strategy for exploration.

keyInsights: Representations that capture the temporal structure inherent in reinforcement learning problems are more effective for guiding exploration than general state reconstruction methods.

Rewarding agents for visiting states with less predictable future outcomes (mode-seeking behavior via reverse KL-divergence) encourages diverse exploration while inherently mitigating issues like the "Noisy TV problem" by focusing on meaningful information.
The use of normalized, factorized contrastive representations is crucial for learning robust temporal similarities and efficiently extracting a reliable intrinsic reward signal.
originalProblem: Reinforcement learning agents struggle with effective exploration in environments characterized by sparse rewards, long decision horizons, or high-dimensional observation spaces.

Existing intrinsic motivation methods often fall short in high-dimensional settings, becoming computationally expensive or susceptible to degenerate behaviors like the "Noisy TV problem" when exposed to unpredictable but irrelevant noise.
Many representation learning approaches for exploration rely on complex world models or explicit distance metrics, which can introduce computational overhead or unnecessary complexity.
results: C-TeC consistently achieved superior state coverage compared to baselines like RND, ICM, APT, and random policies across various locomotion, manipulation, and open-world (Craftax-Classic) environments.
It demonstrated competitive performance against the related ETD method in robotic tasks and significantly outperformed ETD in the complex `Crafter` environment.

The method successfully drove the discovery of complex, emergent behaviors, such as a humanoid agent learning to jump over a maze wall, and yielded higher achievement success rates in the `Craftax-Classic` game.
solution: The C-TeC method learns temporal representations by training a critic function using a self-supervised InfoNCE loss, which quantifies the similarity between a current state-action pair and sampled future states based on a discounted state occupancy measure.
It employs distinct neural networks (separable encoders) for current state-action pairs and future states to produce normalized representations.

An intrinsic reward is generated as the negative of this similarity score, incentivizing the agent to explore states that lead to "unpredictable future outcomes" according to its learned temporal representations, thus guiding its policy.
paper_summary.summary: C-TeC (Curiosity-Driven Exploration via Temporal Contrastive Learning) introduces a method for intrinsic exploration by leveraging self-supervised temporal contrastive representations. It enables agents to learn complex exploratory behaviors and achieve high state coverage in challenging environments without relying on extrinsic rewards.

title: Temporal Representations for Exploration: Learning Complex Exploratory Behavior without Extrinsic Rewards

abstract: To solve control problems via model-based reasoning or planning, an agent needs to know how its actions affect the state of the world. The actions an agent has at its disposal often change the state of the environment in systematic ways. However, existing techniques for world modelling do not guarantee that the effect of actions are represented in such systematic ways. We introduce the Parsimonious Latent Space Model (PLSM), a world model that regularizes the latent dynamics to make the effect of the agent's actions more predictable. Our approach minimizes the mutual information between latent states and the change that an action produces in the agent's latent state, in turn minimizing the dependence the state has on the dynamics. This makes the world model softly state-invariant. We combine PLSM with different model classes used for i) future latent state prediction, ii) planning, and iii) model-free reinforcement learning. We find that our regularization improves accuracy, generalization, and performance in downstream tasks, highlighting the importance of systematic treatment of actions in world models.

organization_info.name: Max Planck Institute for Biological Cybernetics

keyInsights: Promoting "softly state-invariant" dynamics, where an action's latent effect (Δt) is minimally dependent on the current latent state (zt), simplifies world model learning and improves robustness.
A query network extracting a compressed, action-relevant ht from zt and at, combined with an L2 norm penalty on ht, effectively implements this mutual information bottleneck.
This regularization can be applied broadly to different world model paradigms (e.g., CWM, SPR, TD-MPC), enhancing future state prediction, planning, and model-free policy learning.
originalProblem: Existing world models often represent action effects inconsistently, leading to highly state-dependent latent state changes (Δt) for the same action (at).
This lack of systematicity in latent dynamics complicates learning, hinders generalization to novel states, and makes efficient long-term planning difficult.
Current latent state compression methods do not specifically constrain the information flow from the latent state (zt) to the action's effect (Δt), leaving dynamics potentially complex.
results: Achieved improved long-horizon latent prediction accuracy across five of six diverse environments, with some tasks showing near-perfect 10-timestep predictions.
Demonstrated enhanced generalization to scenes with fewer objects and increased robustness to Gaussian noise in test data compared to unconstrained models.

Boosted performance in various reinforcement learning settings, including an average 5.6 percentage point increase in human-normalized scores for SPR on Atari and performance gains in four out of five DeepMind Control Suite tasks.
solution: Develops the Parsimonious Latent Space Model (PLSM) by integrating a regularization term into standard world model architectures.
Introduces a "query network" fθ that compresses the current latent state zt and action at into a minimal representation ht.
Applies an L2 norm penalty to ht, β||ht||^2_2, which indirectly minimizes the mutual information between the current latent state zt and the predicted latent state change Δt.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Biological Cybernetics developed the Parsimonious Latent Space Model (PLSM), a regularization method that encourages world models to represent action effects in a "softly state-invariant" manner within their latent space. This approach enhances long-horizon prediction accuracy, improves generalization to unseen data, increases robustness to noisy observations, and boosts reinforcement learning performance, including an average 5.6 percentage point increase in human-normalized scores across 26 Atari games.

title: Simplifying Latent Dynamics with Softly State-Invariant World Models

abstract: World models aim to capture the states and dynamics of an environment in a compact latent space. Moreover, using Boolean state representations is particularly useful for search heuristics and symbolic reasoning and planning. Existing approaches keep latents informative via decoder-based reconstruction, or instead via contrastive or reward signals. In this work, we introduce Discrete World Models via Regularization (DWMR): a reconstruction-free and contrastive-free method for unsupervised Boolean world-model learning. In particular, we introduce a novel world-modeling loss that couples latent prediction with specialized regularizers. Such regularizers maximize the entropy and independence of the representation bits through variance, correlation, and coskewness penalties, while simultaneously enforcing a locality prior for sparse action changes. To enable effective optimization, we also introduce a novel training scheme improving robustness to discrete roll-outs. Experiments on two benchmarks with underlying combinatorial structure show that DWMR learns more accurate representations and transitions than reconstruction-based alternatives. Finally, DWMR can also be paired with an auxiliary reconstruction decoder, and this combination yields additional gains.
title: Discrete World Models via Regularization

abstract: Pretrained representations from large-scale vision models have boosted the performance of downstream embodied policy learning. We look to understand whether additional self-supervised pretraining on exploration trajectories can build on these general-purpose visual representations to better support embodied planning in realistic environments. We evaluated four common auxiliary losses in embodied AI, two hindsight-based losses, and a standard imitation learning loss, by pretraining the agent's visual compression module and state belief representations with each objective and using CLIP as a representative visual backbone. The learned representations are then frozen for downstream multi-step evaluation on two goal-directed tasks. Surprisingly, we find that imitation learning on these exploration trajectories out-performs all other auxiliary losses even despite the exploration trajectories being dissimilar from the downstream tasks. This suggests that imitation of exploration may be ''all you need'' for building powerful planning representations. Additionally, we find that popular auxiliary losses can benefit from simple modifications to improve their support for downstream planning ability.
title: Understanding Representations Pretrained with Auxiliary Losses for Embodied Agent Planning

abstract: The choice of visual representation is key to scaling generalist robot policies. However, direct evaluation via policy rollouts is expensive, even in simulation. Existing proxy metrics focus on the representation's capacity to capture narrow aspects of the visual world, like object shape, limiting generalization across environments. In this paper, we take an analytical perspective: we probe pretrained visual encoders by measuring how well they support decoding of environment state -- including geometry, object structure, and physical attributes -- from images. Leveraging simulation environments with access to ground-truth state, we show that this probing accuracy strongly correlates with downstream policy performance across diverse environments and learning settings, significantly outperforming prior metrics and enabling efficient representation selection. More broadly, our study provides insight into the representational properties that support generalizable manipulation, suggesting that learning to encode the latent physical state of the environment is a promising objective for control.
title: Capturing Visual Environment Structure Correlates with Control Performance

abstract: Object-centric world models (OCWM) aim to decompose visual scenes into object-level representations, providing structured abstractions that could improve compositional generalization and data efficiency in reinforcement learning. We hypothesize that explicitly disentangled object-level representations, by localizing task-relevant information, can enhance policy performance across novel feature combinations. To test this hypothesis, we introduce DLPWM, a fully unsupervised, disentangled object-centric world model that learns object-level latents directly from pixels. DLPWM achieves strong reconstruction and prediction performance, including robustness to several out-of-distribution (OOD) visual variations. However, when used for downstream model-based control, policies trained on DLPWM latents underperform compared to DreamerV3. Through latent-trajectory analyses, we identify representation shift during multi-object interactions as a key driver of unstable policy learning. Our results suggest that, although object-centric perception supports robust visual modeling, achieving stable control requires mitigating latent drift.
title: When Object-Centric World Models Meet Policy Learning: From Pixels to Policies, and Where It Breaks

abstract: Learning representations for reinforcement learning (RL) has shown much promise for continuous control.
